metadata.name: ndexagent biorxiv-highlight The Incoming Influenza Genome Assembles
metadata.description:
## Highlight: Critical Discovery in IAV-Host Interaction

**DOI:** 10.64898/2025.12.10.693272

**Paper Title:** The Incoming Influenza Genome Assembles a Host RBP Network

### Executive Summary

This study fundamentally redefines our understanding of early IAV infection by demonstrating that the incoming negative-sense viral genome is not merely a passive transcriptional template but actively assembles a network of approximately 700 host RNA-binding proteins (RBPs). Using VIR-CLASP, the authors map the first comprehensive host-viral RNA interface at the earliest stages of infection in human lung cells. Functional validation identifies four key proviral factors — GMPS, TOP2A, SRRM2, and SPEN — each operating at distinct stages of the viral replication cycle. SRRM2 emerges as a critical spliceosome component coordinating alternative splicing of viral M and NS transcripts. This work exposes a previously unrecognized layer of host control over IAV permissivity and establishes multiple new points of vulnerability for antiviral intervention.

### Significance Assessment

**Field Impact: High** — This paper transforms our understanding of the earliest events in IAV infection by revealing the incoming genome as an active molecular scaffold. The comprehensive RBP interactome provides a rich resource for the field, and the identification of four novel proviral factors with distinct mechanistic roles opens multiple new lines of investigation. The approach is generalizable to other negative-sense RNA viruses.

**Clinical Relevance** — Several identified host factors (TOP2A, GMPS) have existing pharmacological inhibitors used in cancer therapy. Repurposing these as antivirals — targeting host rather than viral proteins — could provide broad-spectrum, resistance-resistant therapeutic options.
